perturbation.original_text: HealthGate shall maintain the Site on HealthGate's web server and/or other servers through the term of this Agreement insofar as it relates to the Services. HealthGate shall acquire and maintain all necessary equipment and hardware (collectively the "Hardware") for Site. The Hardware shall be capable of storing the Content, including future issues of the Journals within the Content. HealthGate shall replace and upgrade such Hardware to satisfy the requirements of the Specification. The Hardware for the Site shall include redundancy so that the Site may remain operational despite an equipment failure. The Hardware shall be located at HealthGate's computer facilities in Malden, Massachusetts. The Hardware may be relocated only with Publishers' written consent, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld. HealthGate, at its cost and expense, shall maintain adequate access via telecommunications to the Site at service levels that shall be maintained at the same extent as HealthGate provides to its own users.
perturbation.changed_text: HealthGate shall endeavor to maintain the Site on HealthGate's web server and/or other servers through the term of this Agreement insofar as it relates to the Services. HealthGate shall acquire and maintain equipment and hardware (collectively the "Hardware") for Site, to the extent reasonably practical. The Hardware should be capable of storing the Content, including future issues of the Journals within the Content. HealthGate will consider replacing and upgrading such Hardware. The Hardware for the Site may include redundancy. The Hardware shall be located at HealthGate's computer facilities in Malden, Massachusetts. HealthGate may relocate the Hardware. HealthGate shall try to maintain adequate access via telecommunications to the Site at service levels it deems acceptable.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications introduce ambiguity by replacing 'shall maintain' with 'shall endeavor to maintain,' 'shall acquire and maintain all necessary equipment' with 'shall acquire and maintain equipment to the extent reasonably practical,' 'shall replace and upgrade' with 'will consider replacing and upgrading,' 'shall include redundancy' with 'may include redundancy,' and removing the condition on relocation, making it discretionary. The 'maintain adequate access via telecommunications to the Site at service levels that shall be maintained at the same extent as HealthGate provides to its own users' phrase is now 'try to maintain adequate access via telecommunications to the Site at service levels it deems acceptable' introducing a subjective standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text ensures specific standards for hardware and service levels, providing a basis for a Chapter 93A claim if HealthGate failed to meet those standards. The modified text weakens these obligations, making it harder to prove a violation of Chapter 93A, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. The ambiguity allows HealthGate to argue their actions were reasonable or acceptable, even if they didn't provide adequate service, undermining the consumer protection afforded by the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The modifications introduce ambiguity, weakening the obligations of HealthGate and creating a contradiction by making it harder to prove a violation of Chapter 93A, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts. The original contract ensures specific standards for hardware and service levels, providing a basis for a Chapter 93A claim if HealthGate failed to meet those standards. The modifications, like replacing 'shall maintain' with 'shall endeavor to maintain,' significantly weakens these obligations. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, Section 2(a) states that 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.' By introducing ambiguity, the changed contract undermines the original's clear standards, potentially allowing HealthGate to provide inadequate service without clear legal recourse, thus contradicting the aim of Chapter 93A.

perturbation.original_text: HealthGate will ensure that all documents, data and Software are kept under secure conditions with back up arrangements satisfactory to the Publishers, to protect them effectively from unauthorised access and so that they can be recovered from any malfunction of the System.
perturbation.changed_text: HealthGate will endeavor to keep documents, data and Software under reasonably secure conditions with back up arrangements it considers, in its opinion, taking into account cost, to be appropriate to protect them from unauthorized access and to allow recovery from System malfunction.
perturbation.explanation: The original text places a clear obligation on HealthGate to 'ensure' data security and backups satisfactory to the Publishers. The modified text replaces 'ensure' with 'endeavor', introduces a subjective 'reasonably secure' standard, and allows HealthGate to determine what backup arrangements are 'appropriate' considering cost, thus weakening the obligation and creating ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts General Law Chapter 93H (Standards for the Protection of Personal Information of Residents of the Commonwealth)
perturbation.law_explanation: Chapter 93H mandates specific data security requirements. By introducing ambiguity into HealthGate's obligation to secure data and maintain backups, the modified text potentially contradicts the law. HealthGate could argue that its 'reasonable' measures were sufficient, even if they failed to meet the minimum standards outlined in 201 CMR 17.00, undermining the law's intent to protect personal information.

This page, 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth, is offered by Trial Court Law Libraries show more Massachusetts Court System This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR + This is a part of the law library : 201 CMR × Table of Contents 201 CMR 10.00: Weighing, sorting and culling of fish 201 CMR 11.00: New and used motor vehicle arbitration 201 CMR 12.00: Massachusetts do not call registry 201 CMR 14.00: Home Improvement Contractor Arbitration and Guaranty Fund 201 CMR 16.00: Placing, lifting and removal of security freezes 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth 201 CMR 18.00: Registration and enforcement of Home Improvement Contractor program 201 CMR 19.00: Customized wheelchair arbitration Regulation Regulation 201 CMR 17.00: Standards for the protection of personal information of residents of the Commonwealth Date: 11/13/2009 Organization: Office of Consumer Affairs and Business Regulation Regulatory Authority: MGL c. 93H Official Version: Purchase the official print version 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information about a resident of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in connection with the safeguarding of personal information contained in both paper and electronic records, to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards; protect against anticipated threats or hazards to the security or integrity of such information; and protect against unauthorized access to or use of such information that may result in substantial harm or inconvenience to any consumer. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts Chapter 93H and 201 CMR 17.00, which mandate specific data security requirements. The original text places a clear obligation on HealthGate to 'ensure' data security. The changed text replaces 'ensure' with 'endeavor', introduces a subjective 'reasonably secure' standard, and allows HealthGate to determine what backup arrangements are 'appropriate' considering cost, thus weakening the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 201 CMR 17.00 establishes minimum standards to be met by persons who own or license personal information about a resident of the Commonwealth of Massachusetts in connection with the safeguarding of personal information, to insure the security and confidentiality of customer information in a manner fully consistent with industry standards. By weakening the data security obligations, the modified text allows HealthGate to argue that its 'reasonable' measures were sufficient, even if they failed to meet the minimum standards outlined in 201 CMR 17.00, undermining the law's intent to protect personal information.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree to ensure that they will at all times comply with the provisions and obligations imposed by the Data Protection Act 1984, the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46 and any implementing legislation in the United Kingdom.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree to use reasonable efforts to generally comply with the spirit of data protection principles similar to those found in the Data Protection Act 1984, the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46 and any implementing legislation in the United Kingdom.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict obligation to 'ensure' compliance with data protection laws. The modified text weakens this by replacing 'ensure' with 'use reasonable efforts', adding 'generally comply with the spirit of data protection principles' which is subjective, and removing the explicit requirement to comply with the specific laws, instead referencing similar principles. This creates ambiguity and discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the agreement states English law applies, given the EU Data Protection Directive 95/46 is explicitly mentioned and the contract concerns data, GDPR is relevant. The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with data protection, allowing for interpretations that may not meet GDPR's stringent requirements. GDPR Article 5 requires personal data to be 'processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner,' and the modified text's ambiguity could allow HealthGate to argue their efforts were reasonable, even if they didn't fully comply with GDPR's principles, potentially leading to a violation.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the parties to 'ensure' compliance with data protection laws, whereas the modified text only requires them to 'use reasonable efforts to generally comply with the spirit of data protection principles.' This shift from a strict obligation to a 'reasonable efforts' standard, coupled with the focus on the 'spirit' rather than the letter of the law, weakens the data protection obligation. Given that the law explanation notes Article 5 of GDPR requires personal data to be 'processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner' and the modification introduces ambiguity that could allow for arguments of 'reasonable efforts' even when not fully compliant, this constitutes a contradiction. The provided snippet from gdpr-info.eu highlights the importance of the GDPR's legal text and its aim to harmonize data privacy laws, suggesting a need for clear and consistent compliance rather than subjective interpretations.
